title: How to: generate hundreds of maps by combining QGIS with Python (code included!)
content:
At this year’s Dataharvest I delivered a workshop on using Python in QGIS to automate the process of exporting maps for multiple locations. Here’s how to do it (you can find a GitHub repository with materials and links here).

Making a map for a story is cool — but what if you could make a map for every reader? Or if you’re working on a project involving teams in different regions or countries, what if you could give each one of those teams a map centred on their own patch?

Normally you would have to manually move the map to centre it on a key city, and then export an image. Then do it again and again and again for every area.

Luckily, QGIS has the ability to run code. And this is a great excuse to start using it.

Before getting into the code, however, you need a map for the code to move around

Create a new project in QGIS and import any shape file(s) that you want to show on your map, along with a base map underneath that. I’ve created a tutorial here which imports flood defences and then adds an OpenStreetMap base layer underneath those.

creator: Paul Bradshaw
sourceDescription: Comment, analysis and links covering online journalism and online news, citizen journalism, blogging, vlogging, photoblogging, podcasts, vodcasts, interactive storytelling, publishing, Computer Assisted Reporting, User Generated Content, searching and all things internet.
